intro: New Zealand's weather varies dramatically by region and month. This matrix shows the best time to visit each destination — based on actual conditions, not just averages.

destinations.name: Queenstown

notes.Jun: Ski season opens — great for snow activities
notes.Jul: Peak ski season — best snow base, busiest month
notes.Aug: Good skiing, crowds thinning late month
notes.Dec: Long days, warm — but busy and expensive
notes.Jan: Peak summer — great weather, very busy

destinations.summary: Summer (Dec–Feb) for outdoor adventure and water sports. Winter (Jun–Aug) for skiing. Spring and autumn are quieter and cheaper.

destinations.name: Rotorua

notes.Jan: Hot and humid — perfect for hot pools and outdoor activities
notes.Feb: Best month — warm, settled, not peak crowding
notes.Jun: Cold but geothermal appeal all year; indoor activities good
notes.Jul: Winter school holidays — busy despite cold weather
notes.Nov: Warming up, spring blooms, good value

destinations.summary: Year-round destination but best November–March. Geothermal attractions work in all weather. Summer is hot and busy; winter is cold but fine for indoor activities.

destinations.name: Bay of Islands

notes.Jan: Peak season — warm seas, dolphin cruises daily, book early
notes.Feb: Often the best month — warm, settled, slightly fewer crowds
notes.Jun: Cold and rainy — many operators reduce services
notes.Jul: School holidays bring crowds despite cold weather
notes.Dec: Christmas rush — most expensive week of the year
notes.Oct: Spring warming up, whale watching season begins

destinations.summary: Strictly a summer destination. Best December–March. Avoid May–August — cold, wet, and many boat operators cut back services.

destinations.name: Coromandel

notes.Jan: Summer peak — Hot Water Beach, Cathedral Cove. Book 3+ months ahead.
notes.Feb: Often quieter than Jan — still warm, great beaches
notes.Jun: Very quiet — most holiday accommodation closed or restricted
notes.Jul: School holidays create brief spike, otherwise very quiet
notes.Oct: Good shoulder season — warm enough, uncrowded

destinations.summary: Classic NZ summer destination. Best December–February for beaches. Off-season (May–Aug) it's very quiet — some facilities close.

destinations.name: Auckland

notes.Jan: Hot but can be humid and rainy — indoor backup plans useful
notes.Jun: Coolest and wettest month — still plenty to do indoors
notes.Jul: School holidays bring families to Auckland Zoo, MOTAT etc
notes.Feb: Often the most settled month of Auckland summer

destinations.summary: Year-round city destination. Warmest and most settled November–April. Rainy days are common year-round — good range of indoor attractions makes it weatherproof.

destinations.name: Wellington

notes.Jan: Warm and often sunny — Te Papa, waterfront walks excellent
notes.Jun: Wettest month — still great for Te Papa, Zealandia, cafes
notes.Jul: Winter school holidays — families flock to museums
notes.Nov: Spring settling in — good combination of weather and value

destinations.summary: Wellington is famous for wind and rain. Best January–March. Strong museum and cafe culture makes it viable year-round — but avoid June–August if you want outdoor time.

destinations.name: Christchurch

notes.Jan: Hot and sunny — excellent for the gondola, Hagley Park, beaches
notes.Feb: Often the most settled month — good value
notes.Jun: Cold and grey — good for art galleries and Willowbank
notes.Dec: Busy but great weather — good base for South Island road trips

destinations.summary: Best November–March. Christchurch can be cold and grey in winter. Summer sees lovely long days and good access to Canterbury Plains and ski areas.

destinations.name: Wanaka

notes.Jul: Peak ski season — Cardrona and Treble Cone at their best
notes.Jun: Ski season opens — quieter and cheaper than Queenstown
notes.Jan: Summer peak — lake swimming, Roy's Peak track, Puzzling World
notes.Feb: Often the best summer month — warm, settled, fewer crowds than Jan

destinations.summary: Two seasons: Summer (Dec–Mar) for lake activities and hiking; Winter (Jun–Aug) for skiing at Cardrona and Treble Cone. Shoulder seasons are quiet with limited services.

destinations.name: Nelson / Abel Tasman

notes.Jan: Sunniest region in NZ — Abel Tasman coast track, golden beaches
notes.Feb: Excellent — warm, settled, Abel Tasman at its best
notes.Jun: Cold and quiet — Abel Tasman water taxis still run
notes.Jul: School holidays bring families — still cold for swimming
notes.Nov: Spring warming — great shoulder season value

destinations.summary: Nelson consistently gets the most sunshine hours in NZ. Best November–April for the Abel Tasman coast. Winter is cold but mild by South Island standards.

destinations.name: Taupo

notes.Jan: Hot — great for lake swimming, Huka Falls, Skydive
notes.Jul: School holiday week sees Taupo busier than usual
notes.Jun: Cold — thermal hot pools especially popular
notes.Feb: Summer peak — long days, lake activities in full swing

destinations.summary: Year-round destination with good indoor/outdoor balance. Summer best for lake activities. Winter is cold but Taupo's geothermal hot pools are a draw year-round.

destinations.name: Fiordland

notes.Jan: Busiest month but great weather — book Milford cruise months ahead
notes.Feb: Often less crowded than Jan — still excellent
notes.Apr: Autumn colours, fewer tourists, still good weather
notes.Jun: Very wet and very cold — but the waterfalls are spectacular
notes.Jul: Quietest month — very wet, limited services, but dramatic scenery

destinations.summary: Fiordland is one of the wettest places on Earth — rain actually enhances the waterfalls. Best November–April. The Milford Sound cruise runs year-round but is most comfortable in summer.

destinations.name: Marlborough

notes.Jan: Hot and sunny — Marlborough Sounds by boat, wine tours
notes.Feb: Harvest season — wineries busiest, great atmosphere
notes.Dec: Summer starting — Sounds still uncrowded relative to Jan

destinations.summary: Best January–March. Marlborough is one of NZ's sunniest regions but has cold winters. The Sounds are spectacular year-round by water but summer is best for swimming.

destinations.name: Northland

notes.Jan: Peak — 90 Mile Beach, Cape Reinga, Te Paki sand dunes
notes.Feb: Often the best month — still hot but less crowded
notes.Jun: Rainy season — Cape Reinga tours still run but cold
notes.Jul: School holidays bring families despite cooler weather
notes.Oct: Pohutukawa trees begin blooming — beautiful drive north

destinations.summary: Best December–April. Northland has warm subtropical weather in summer. The Far North (Cape Reinga) is an iconic NZ family road trip but most enjoyable in warm months.

destinations.name: Hawke's Bay

notes.Jan: Hot and sunny — Cape Kidnappers, beach, wine country
notes.Feb: Excellent — Art Deco weekend mid-month, great weather
notes.Jun: Cold and wet — most outdoor activities limited
notes.Mar: Harvest season — great for foodies and families

destinations.summary: Best December–March. One of NZ's warmest, sunniest regions. Summer days are long and hot — great for the beach, cycling, and cape gannet colony visits.

faqs.q: What is the best month to visit New Zealand with kids?
faqs.a: January and February are consistently the best months across most of NZ — warm, long days, most activities running. December is peak season but more expensive and crowded. October–November is the best shoulder season for value-conscious families.

faqs.q: Is New Zealand worth visiting in winter with kids?
faqs.a: Yes — especially if you're ski-focused. Queenstown and Wanaka are excellent for skiing in July–August. For non-ski families, winter is much quieter and cheaper, though the South Island in particular gets cold. Rotorua, Auckland and Wellington work well year-round.

faqs.q: What is the cheapest time to visit New Zealand?
faqs.a: May–September (excluding school holiday weeks in July) offers the cheapest accommodation and campervan rates. Shoulder seasons — October–November and March–April — offer a good balance of decent weather and lower prices.

faqs.q: Does New Zealand have four seasons?
faqs.a: Yes. Summer (December–February), Autumn (March–May), Winter (June–August), Spring (September–November). The South Island has more extreme seasons — colder winters, hotter summers in central/eastern areas. The North Island is milder year-round.

faqs.q: When does it rain most in New Zealand?
faqs.a: It depends heavily on region. The West Coast of the South Island (Greymouth, Westport) is one of the wettest places on Earth year-round. Northland and Bay of Islands have a distinct wet season June–August. The east coast regions (Hawke's Bay, Marlborough, Canterbury) are drier. Wellington is windy with rain distributed through the year.
